subtyping_example_1.solution:
Example subtyping_example_1 :
  TRcd_kj <: TRcd_j.
Proof with eauto.
  unfold TRcd_kj, TRcd_j.
  apply S_RcdWidth.
Qed.
subtyping_example_1.explanation: A record with more fields is a subtype of one with fewer fields (width subtyping). {k:A->A, j:B->B} <: {j:B->B} by dropping the k field.

subtyping_example_2.solution:
Example subtyping_example_2 :
  <{{ Top -> TRcd_kj }}> <:
          <{{ (C -> C) -> TRcd_j }}>.
Proof with eauto.
  apply S_Arrow.
  - apply S_Top.
  - apply subtyping_example_1.
Qed.
subtyping_example_2.explanation: Arrow subtyping: contravariant in argument (C->C <: Top by S_Top), covariant in return (TRcd_kj <: TRcd_j by width).

subtyping_example_3.solution:
Example subtyping_example_3 :
  <{{ nil -> (j : A :: nil) }}> <:
          <{{ (k : B :: nil) -> nil }}>.
Proof with eauto.
  apply S_Arrow.
  - apply S_RcdWidth.
  - apply S_RcdWidth.
Qed.
subtyping_example_3.explanation: Arrow subtyping with records: {} <: {k:B} (width subtyping, reversed for contravariance) and {j:A} <: {} (width subtyping).

subtyping_example_4.solution:
Example subtyping_example_4 :
  <{{ x : A :: y : B :: z : C :: nil }}> <:
  <{{ z : C :: y : B :: x : A :: nil }}>.
Proof with eauto.
  apply S_Trans with <{{ y : B :: x : A :: z : C :: nil }}>.
  - apply S_RcdPerm; try auto. discriminate.
  - apply S_Trans with <{{ y : B :: z : C :: x : A :: nil }}>.
    + apply S_RcdDepth. apply S_Refl.
      apply S_RcdPerm; try auto. discriminate.
    + apply S_RcdPerm; try auto. discriminate.
Qed.
subtyping_example_4.explanation: Record permutation: fields can be reordered using S_RcdPerm. Multiple permutation steps get from one order to another.

rcd_types_match_informal.solution:
(* Manual grade exercise.
   Informal proof of rcd_types_match:
   If S <: T and Tlookup i T = Some Ti, then there exists Si such that
   Tlookup i S = Some Si and Si <: Ti.
   
   Proof: By induction on the subtyping derivation S <: T.
   - S_Refl: S = T, so Si = Ti and Si <: Ti by reflexivity.
   - S_Trans: S <: U <: T. By IH on U <: T, get Ui with Tlookup i U = Some Ui and Ui <: Ti.
     Then by IH on S <: U, get Si with Tlookup i S = Some Si and Si <: Ui.
     By transitivity, Si <: Ti.
   - S_RcdWidth: T has fewer fields than S. Since i is in T, it's also in S.
   - S_RcdDepth: S and T have the same field names with S's types being subtypes.
   - S_RcdPerm: Fields are permuted; lookup is by name so order doesn't matter. *)
rcd_types_match_informal.explanation: By induction on the subtyping derivation, showing that subtyping preserves field lookup.

sub_inversion_arrow.solution:
Lemma sub_inversion_arrow : forall U V1 V2,
     U <: <{{ V1 -> V2 }}> ->
     exists U1 U2,
       (U= <{{ U1 -> U2 }}> ) /\ (V1 <: U1) /\ (U2 <: V2).
Proof with eauto.
  intros U V1 V2 Hs.
  remember <{{ V1 -> V2 }}> as V.
  generalize dependent V2. generalize dependent V1.
  induction Hs; intros; try discriminate.
  - (* S_Refl *) injection HeqV as H1 H2. subst.
    exists V1, V2. repeat split; apply S_Refl.
  - (* S_Trans *)
    destruct (IHHs2 V1 V2 HeqV) as [U1 [U2 [HeqU [HV1 HU2]]]].
    destruct (IHHs1 U1 U2 HeqU) as [S1 [S2 [HeqS [HU1 HS2]]]].
    exists S1, S2. repeat split.
    + assumption.
    + eapply S_Trans; eassumption.
    + eapply S_Trans; eassumption.
  - (* S_Arrow *) injection HeqV as H1 H2. subst.
    exists S1, S2. repeat split; assumption.
Qed.
sub_inversion_arrow.explanation: If U is a subtype of an arrow type, then U must also be an arrow type with appropriate subtyping on the components.

typing_example_0.solution:
Example typing_example_0 :
  empty |-- <{ (\z:A, z) }> \in <{ A -> A }>.
Proof.
  apply T_Abs. apply T_Var. reflexivity.
Qed.
typing_example_0.explanation: The identity function on type A has type A -> A.

typing_example_1.solution:
Example typing_example_1 :
  empty |-- <{ (\x:(Top -> B -> B), x) (\z:A, z) }> \in <{ Top -> B -> B }>.
Proof.
  eapply T_App.
  - apply T_Abs. apply T_Var. reflexivity.
  - eapply T_Sub.
    + apply T_Abs. apply T_Var. reflexivity.
    + apply S_Arrow. apply S_Top. apply S_Arrow. apply S_Refl. apply S_Refl.
Qed.
typing_example_1.explanation: Apply the identity function at type Top->B->B to (\z:A.z), using subsumption to match the argument type.

typing_example_2.solution:
Example typing_example_2 :
  empty |-- <{ (\x:Top, x) ((\z:A, z), (\z:B, z)) }> \in <{ Top }>.
Proof.
  eapply T_App.
  - apply T_Abs. apply T_Var. reflexivity.
  - eapply T_Sub.
    + eapply T_RCons; eauto.
    + apply S_Top.
Qed.
typing_example_2.explanation: Apply the identity at Top to a record value, using subsumption to lift the record type to Top.

canonical_forms_of_arrow_types.solution:
Lemma canonical_forms_of_arrow_types : forall Gamma s T1 T2,
  Gamma |-- s \in <{{ T1 -> T2 }}> ->
  value s ->
  exists x S1 s2,
    s = <{ \x:S1, s2 }>.
Proof.
  intros Gamma s T1 T2 Ht Hv.
  remember <{{ T1 -> T2 }}> as T.
  generalize dependent T2. generalize dependent T1.
  induction Ht; intros; try solve [inversion Hv; discriminate].
  - (* T_Abs *) exists x, T1, t1. reflexivity.
  - (* T_Sub *) eapply IHHt; try assumption.
    apply sub_inversion_arrow in H.
    destruct H as [U1 [U2 [HeqU _]]]. assumption.
Qed.
canonical_forms_of_arrow_types.explanation: A value of arrow type must be an abstraction. The subsumption case uses sub_inversion_arrow.
